mechanism_narrative: Consistent growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates labor market strength, which drives consumer spending and corporate earnings, leading to positive S&P 500 returns over the next trading month. This signal is always active when PAYEMS increases from the prior reading.
failure_condition: If PAYEMS increases month-over-month but S&P 500 forward 20-day returns are negative in more than 60% of such instances over a 3-year rolling window, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) exceeds 4.5%, the cost of capital rises sharply, compressing equity valuations and dampening economic activity, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 20 trading days while the rate remains above the threshold.
failure_condition: If over any 2-year period where FEDFUNDS > 4.5%, the average 20-day forward SPX return is positive or not significantly different from periods below 4.5% (t-test p < 0.05), the hypothesis is falsified.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneously high inflation (CPIAUCSL) and low unemployment (UNRATE) tighten labor markets and force the Fed to raise rates, creating a stagflationary risk that weighs on equity valuations. The interaction between price pressures and labor tightness amplifies the negative return signal.
failure_condition: If months when both CPIAUCSL is in the top quartile and UNRATE is in the bottom quartile produce positive 20-day forward SPX returns in more than 40% of cases, the hypothesis fails.

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production (INDPRO) grows at an annualized rate above 3%, it signals capacity expansion and improving supply conditions. In a supply-constrained expansion regime, this leads to positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next quarter as profit margins benefit from output growth.
failure_condition: If in instances where INDPRO annualized growth exceeds 3%, the 60-day forward SPX return is negative in over 50% of observations, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Rising retail sales (RSAFS) reflect robust consumer demand, which supports corporate revenues and earnings growth. This fundamental tailwind consistently drives positive S&P 500 returns over the following 20 trading days irrespective of other macro conditions.
failure_condition: If month-over-month increases in RSAFS are followed by negative 20-day SPX returns in more than 40% of cases over a 5-year period, the hypothesis is falsified.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) above 1,500 thousand units monthly signal robust residential investment and consumer confidence, predicting strong economic momentum. This threshold-activation leads to positive S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 60 trading days as housing activity ripples through the economy.
failure_condition: If months with HOUST above 1,500 are followed by 60-day SPX returns that are negative more than 30% of the time, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) is below 3% and the unemployment rate (UNRATE) is below 4%, the economy operates in a 'Goldilocks' zone where accommodative policy meets labor tightness without overheating. This joint condition produces positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days as the macro backdrop is ideal for risk assets.
failure_condition: If in any 3-year window the joint condition (FEDFUNDS<3% and UNRATE<4%) yields negative average 20-day SPX returns, the interaction hypothesis is falsified.

mechanism_narrative: In a monetary tightening cycle where the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) has increased by 50bp or more over the prior three months, additional rate hikes or elevated rate levels contract equity valuations. This regime-conditional mechanism predicts negative S&P 500 forward returns over the subsequent 5 trading days as markets price in tighter conditions.
failure_condition: If during tightening cycles (as defined) the 5-day forward SPX return is positive more than 45% of the time, the regime-conditional directional prediction is falsified.
